setup: I asked a privacy advocate what they got for Christmas.
punchline: They said they couldn't tell me, but the metadata suggests it was from Amazon, weighed 2kg, and arrived on the 23rd.

setup: What do open-source developers and magicians have in common?
punchline: They both show you exactly how the trick works and you still can't do it yourself.

setup: My VPN provider promises 'zero logs.'
punchline: Which is also the number of times I've been able to verify that.

setup: I decentralised my marriage.
punchline: Now every major decision requires consensus from at least three of five trusted nodes. We've never been happier. Or made a decision.

setup: The government says if you've nothing to hide, you've nothing to fear.
punchline: And yet they classified 50 million documents last year. Must be terrified.

setup: My friend said sovereign computing means never having to say you're sorry.
punchline: That's not true. I apologise to my home server every single morning.

setup: I tried to explain end-to-end encryption to my mum.
punchline: She said 'So it's like whispering?' I said 'Yes, if whispering involved prime numbers the size of Wales.'

setup: How many cypherpunks does it take to change a lightbulb?
punchline: None. They'd rather sit in the dark than let the power company know which room they're in.

setup: Self-custody is great. I'm my own bank now.
punchline: Unfortunately, my bank has terrible opening hours, no customer support, and the manager keeps losing the passwords.

setup: A surveillance state and a helicopter parent have a lot in common.
punchline: They both read all your messages and insist it's for your own safety.

setup: I told my friend I only use censorship-resistant platforms.
punchline: He said 'That's brilliant, how do I find you?' I said 'Exactly.'

setup: People say open-source software is free as in freedom, not free as in beer.
punchline: But after 14 hours configuring it, you'll definitely need the beer as well.

setup: My password manager holds 487 unique, high-entropy passwords.
punchline: And they're all protected by one password I also use for my luggage.

setup: My Meshtastic node has been running on solar for six months straight.
punchline: It's the most committed relationship I've ever had; it never loses power and it always stays connected.

setup: I told my wife I needed another LoRa radio for the house.
punchline: She said we already have communication problems without adding more nodes to the mesh.

setup: What's the difference between a Meshtastic user and a doomsday prepper?
punchline: The Meshtastic user already has comms sorted and just needs to work on the tinned beans.

setup: My message took 7 hops to reach my mate across town.
punchline: Still faster than him replying to a WhatsApp.

setup: I put a Meshtastic node on my roof, one in the shed, and one on the dog.
punchline: The dog has the best SNR in the neighbourhood. He's a very good node boy.

setup: A Meshtastic firmware update bricked my node last night.
punchline: On the bright side, it's now the perfect paperweight for my off-grid survival plans.

setup: I tried explaining Meshtastic to my parents. 'So it's like texting but without the internet, phone signal, or any infrastructure whatsoever.'
punchline: My dad said, 'So it's like talking to your mother - the message goes out but you never know if it arrived.'

setup: My range test hit 84 kilometres yesterday.
punchline: The message just said 'test'. Eighty-four kilometres of radio engineering to transmit the least interesting word in the English language.

setup: I've got 14 solar-powered Meshtastic nodes deployed across the countryside.
punchline: My electricity bill is zero. My LoRa radio bill, however, suggests I may need an intervention.

setup: The serial chat on my Meshtastic node keeps showing messages from someone called 'NODE_0xDEAD'.
punchline: Either someone's got a dark sense of humour or my mesh network has ghosts. Either way, the SNR is immaculate.

setup: My neighbour asked why I have antennas on every surface of my house.
punchline: I said it's a mesh network. He said it looks like my house is trying to leave the planet. Fair point, the gain on that rooftop Yagi is absurd.

setup: Meshtastic users don't have a fear of missing out.
punchline: We have a fear of missing packets. Completely different anxiety, much worse treatment options.

setup: I accidentally posted my nsec instead of my npub.
punchline: Within thirty seconds I'd zapped myself 50,000 sats, mass-followed every Bitcoin influencer, and changed my display name to 'PWNED_LOL'. Honestly, the new followers were the worst part.

setup: Nostr stands for 'Notes and Other Stuff Transmitted by Relays'.
punchline: The 'other stuff' is mostly people arguing about which client is best. It's like Linux distro wars but everyone's paying each other sats to fight.

setup: My mate submitted a NIP proposal for a new event kind to standardise how people complain about NIP proposals.
punchline: It was rejected. Three people forked it. Two of those forks are now in production on clients nobody uses.

setup: I run my own Nostr relay. It's very exclusive.
punchline: The only users are me, three bots, and someone who accidentally connected while trying to set up their own relay. We're a tight-knit community.

setup: What's the Nostr user's love language?
punchline: Zaps. Nothing says 'I appreciate you' like mass-broadcasting a cryptographically signed Lightning payment to every relay on the planet.

setup: I tried to explain Nostr key management to my nan.
punchline: She said, 'So if I lose this one string of letters, I lose my entire identity forever with no recovery?' I said yes. She said that's just like her Tesco Clubcard situation.

setup: There are now 47 Nostr clients to choose from.
punchline: Each one renders the same note in a slightly different shade of purple and crashes on a completely unique set of event kinds.

setup: A centralised social network bans you. A decentralised social network can't ban you.
punchline: Instead, fifty relay operators individually decide they don't like you. Much more personal.

setup: I set up a Nostr bot that auto-zaps anyone who posts something genuinely original.
punchline: It's been running for three months. My Lightning wallet balance is completely untouched.

setup: My therapist asked me to describe my relationship with social media.
punchline: I said I maintain three npubs, subscribe to 200 relays, wrote two NIPs, and argue about protocol design at 2am. She said, 'So you left Twitter.' I said, 'I evolved.'

setup: I bought a Handshake TLD at auction for 500 HNS.
punchline: Now I own the entire '.cheese' namespace on a blockchain. ICANN could never. My mum says I should have spent the money on actual cheese.

setup: ICANN charges hundreds of thousands to apply for a new TLD. Handshake lets you auction one for pocket change.
punchline: The catch is that only seven people can resolve it, and three of them are the same person on different browsers.

setup: I told a DNS engineer I was decentralising the root zone with blockchain technology.
punchline: He stared at me for a full ten seconds, whispered 'but why', and then just walked into the sea.

setup: Handshake name resolution is trustless, censorship-resistant, and fully decentralised.
punchline: It's also fully optional, which is a polite way of saying almost no browser supports it natively. But apart from that - flawless.

setup: I won a Handshake auction for the TLD '.bitcoin' in 2021.
punchline: I've spent five years waiting for someone to want a domain on it. In the meantime, the name renewal fees have cost me more than the auction. This is what early adoption feels like.

setup: My mate asked me what Bitcoin's difficulty adjustment is.
punchline: I said it's like if Sudoku got harder every time someone in China bought a new calculator. He said 'that doesn't make sense.' I said 'welcome aboard.'

setup: I stored my seed phrase in a fireproof safe, a bank vault, and engraved on titanium plates buried in two separate locations.
punchline: My wife stores her banking password in a note on her phone called 'passwords' and has never once been hacked. I'm starting to think the universe is just mocking me.

setup: The five stages of a Bitcoin halving: denial that it's priced in, anger that it's priced in, bargaining that maybe it's partially priced in, depression as the price drops 20% the week after...
punchline: ...and acceptance, four months later, when you're up 300% and telling everyone you always knew it wasn't priced in.

setup: I finally got my dad to buy Bitcoin. He rang me at 6am the next morning absolutely furious.
punchline: He'd been up all night because he bought at £42,000 and it dropped to £41,800 and he wanted to know which government department to complain to.

setup: My therapist asked me to describe my relationship with Bitcoin in one sentence.
punchline: I said 'I check my phone 200 times a day to watch a number go up or down by amounts that wouldn't even cover lunch, and I have never once taken profit because that would be a taxable event and also a moral failure.' She increased my sessions to twice a week.

setup: A Bitcoin maximalist walks into a bar and orders a beer. The bartender says 'that'll be £5.' He says 'do you accept Lightning?'
punchline: The bartender says no. The maximalist says 'have fun staying poor,' then walks out because he doesn't actually carry cash anymore and his card is maxed out from buying the dip.

setup: People say Bitcoiners are cultish but that's ridiculous. We simply believe one asset will replace all global money, preach it to strangers, have a sacred text by an anonymous founder no one's ever met, celebrate a halving ritual every four years...
punchline: ...use the phrase 'stay humble and stack sats' as a greeting, and put laser eyes on our profile pictures to signal the faithful. Completely normal behaviour.

setup: I tried to orange-pill my nan at Christmas dinner. Forty-five minutes in, she looked me dead in the eye and said:
punchline: 'So let me get this straight, love. Computers do maths puzzles to make magic internet money that you keep on a USB stick you're terrified of losing, and you think the Bank of England is the scam?' And honestly I've never recovered.

setup: The Bitcoin mempool is like an airport during a bank holiday.
punchline: Everyone's queuing, nobody knows why it costs this much, someone's trying to get through with 1 sat/vbyte like they've shown up at Heathrow with a handwritten boarding pass, and the whales are in the priority lane paying £400 to move first. Meanwhile you're sat there watching your transaction for 11 hours thinking 'I could have just used my debit card.'

setup: My cold storage setup: Ledger in a Faraday bag, in a fireproof safe, in a room with a deadbolt, seed phrase split into three Shamir shares stored across two countries.
punchline: Total amount secured: £280 worth of Bitcoin I bought in 2021 that is now worth £140.

setup: Someone told me 'we're still early' in 2013. Then again in 2017. And 2021. And last Tuesday.
punchline: At this point I'm fairly sure my grandchildren will be on their deathbeds in 2090 whispering 'we're still early' to a nurse who does not care.

setup: The worst part about understanding Bitcoin is going to dinner parties.
punchline: Someone mentions crypto and everyone looks at you, and you have two options: say 'Bitcoin isn't crypto' and ruin the evening, or stay silent and physically vibrate for the next three hours while someone explains how their Solana NFT dog is going to revolutionise gaming.

setup: Not your keys, not your coins. Your keys, your crippling responsibility.
punchline: I now have genuine anxiety dreams about house fires, boating accidents, and the possibility that 'orange' was actually 'arrange' on word 7 of my seed phrase. Banks may be the enemy, but at least their customer service line doesn't tell you to 'check the docs.'

setup: I told someone at work that Bitcoin fixes this. They asked what specifically it fixes.
punchline: And I panicked and said 'inflation' even though I'd just spent 40,000 sats on a mass-produced sticker that says 'Bitcoin fixes this' which, adjusting for the exchange rate, cost me roughly £27. So apparently not impulse purchases.

setup: The 'zoom out' crowd are absolutely unhinged. Bitcoin drops 45% and they post a 15-year log chart where the crash is literally one pixel.
punchline: Mate, if I zoom out far enough on a photo of the Titanic it looks like a lovely calm ocean. Doesn't mean the people on board were having a great time.

setup: I rebalanced my Lightning channels for six hours last night. My wife asked if I was having an affair.
punchline: I said 'I wish - at least that would have better inbound liquidity.'

setup: My therapist asked me to describe a healthy relationship. I said 'both sides have balanced capacity, fees are transparent, and neither party force closes without warning.'
punchline: She said 'we should increase our sessions.' I said 'only if you open a channel first.'

setup: A Lightning node operator dies and goes to heaven. St Peter says 'You've lived a good life. Any last request?'
punchline: He says 'Can you check if my payment to that Greek restaurant in 2024 ever resolved? It's been pending for two years and I never got my souvlaki.'

setup: Running a Lightning node is like having a Tamagotchi, except instead of dying from neglect, it force closes your largest channel at 3am on Christmas morning
punchline: and then your peer's watchtower publishes the justice transaction before you've even found your glasses. Happy Christmas, here's your penalty.

setup: I finally got a payment to route on the first try yesterday. No failures. No retries. Just... success.
punchline: I immediately closed the terminal because I knew the simulation was glitching and I didn't want them to patch it.

setup: My node earned 47 satoshis in routing fees last month. That's roughly 3p.
punchline: My electricity bill for running the node was £22. But you don't understand - I'm providing LIQUIDITY to the NETWORK. I am CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE. My wife has started leaving estate agent brochures on the kitchen table.

setup: Submarine swaps sound like an exciting military operation. In reality
punchline: it's a man in his pants at 2am moving sats from one place to slightly different place, paying fees both ways, so his spreadsheet looks tidier. The submarine is his marriage, and it is sinking.

setup: I joined a Ring of Fire to improve my connectivity. Eight strangers, all opening channels to each other in a circle of trust and cooperation.
punchline: Within a week, three had force closed, one disappeared entirely, and the remaining four started a group chat called 'Ring of Trauma' where we mostly just share screenshots of stuck HTLCs and cry.

setup: Force closing a channel is the Lightning Network equivalent of flipping the table during a board game.
punchline: Except the table takes two weeks to hit the floor, everyone can watch it falling on the blockchain, and the cleanup costs you 15,000 sats in on-chain fees. But your HONOUR is intact.

setup: A pleb node runner, a whale node runner, and an LND developer walk into a bar. The pleb says 'I'll have a beer.' The whale says 'I'll buy the bar.' The LND developer says
punchline: 'I'll have whatever you're having, but it'll take 47 database migrations, a config flag marked --reckless, and if the bartender sneezes during pour, the whole pub force closes.'

setup: My Amboss node score went up to 7.2 million yesterday. I texted my dad about it.
punchline: He said 'Is that good?' and honestly that question sent me into a two-hour existential spiral because I realised I have absolutely no idea. Nobody does. We're all just making the number go up and hoping it means something. It's like CrossFit for cryptographers.

setup: You want to know real pain? Real, genuine, soul-crushing pain?
punchline: Opening a perfectly balanced 10 million sat channel with ideal fees, beautiful connectivity, a peer with 99.9% uptime - and then watching them drain it completely to one side in a single payment within forty minutes. That channel is now a decorative ornament. A digital paperweight. A monument to hope.

setup: I explained Lightning Network routing to my grandmother. She listened carefully and said
punchline: 'So you're telling me you lot built a system where you send money through a chain of strangers, any of whom could be offline, and if one of them has the wrong amount in the wrong direction, the whole thing fails and starts over? We had a word for that in my day - it was called the POST OFFICE.'

setup: The five stages of a Lightning force close: Denial - 'my peer will come back online.' Anger - 'that absolute TRAITOR.' Bargaining - 'maybe if I restart my node seventeen times.'
punchline: Depression - watching the timelock tick down for 144 blocks while your sats sit in on-chain purgatory. Acceptance - never comes. I am on force close number twelve and I am still on stage two for ALL of them.

setup: Begging for inbound liquidity is the most humiliating thing a grown adult can do. You're essentially posting in Telegram groups saying 'please, sir, would you consider pointing some of your made-up internet money in my direction so that other people's made-up internet money can flow through my laptop.'
punchline: And when someone finally opens a channel to you, the GRATITUDE you feel is genuinely pathetic. I sent a man in Brazil a thank-you voice note. We're friends now. My wife is jealous of a node operator called xX_SatoshiMaxi_Xx and honestly? She should be. He gave me 5 million sats of inbound. What has she ever done.

setup: My mate said he's mass-adopting Nostr by onboarding his whole family.
punchline: His mum's first post was her nsec. His dad zapped it 500 sats thinking it was a referral code. His nan reported them both to a relay that doesn't have a report button.

setup: I asked a Nostr developer how many NIPs it takes to change a lightbulb.
punchline: Fourteen. NIP-97 defines the bulb schema, NIP-98 handles the rotation event, NIP-99 introduces an optional darkness caveat, and the remaining eleven are competing proposals from people who think the room should stay dark because "that's more aligned with the protocol's original vision."

setup: Nostr is truly decentralised. No single entity controls the network.
punchline: It's controlled by three relays, two clients that kind of work, and one mass DM from Fiatjaf that just says "this is fine."

setup: My therapist asked me to describe my most traumatic experience.
punchline: I told her about the mass key rotation event of 2025, where I lost my npub, recovered the wrong nsec, accidentally signed into someone else's identity, posted "GM" from their account, and got zapped 12,000 sats for a note I never wrote. She referred me to a psychiatrist. He was on Nostr. He'd already seen the thread.

setup: I explained Nostr to my non-technical friend. She asked, "So what do people actually post about?"
punchline: I opened my feed. The first post was about Nostr. The second was about how great Nostr is. The third was someone complaining that people only post about Nostr. The fourth was a screenshot of that complaint, captioned "This is why we need Nostr."

setup: A Nostr user, a CrossFit enthusiast, and a vegan walk into a bar.
punchline: Just kidding, it's the same person and they already told everyone in the bar about all three before sitting down. Then they zapped the bartender and asked if the pub accepts Lightning.

setup: I posted "I'm thinking of leaving Nostr" to see if anyone would care.
punchline: I got 47 zaps, 200 reposts, a mass follow event, three NIP proposals to standardise exit announcements, and a mass DM group called "intervention" that couldn't agree on which client to use for the actual intervention.

setup: I asked my wife to try Nostr. She said she'd think about it.
punchline: Three months later I'd set up her keys, backed up her nsec in four locations, explained the difference between relays and clients, migrated her follow list twice after a relay went down, built her a custom feed, and she posted once. It said "Is this thing on?" Nobody saw it. She went back to Instagram. I've never recovered.

setup: Client diversity on Nostr means freedom of choice.
punchline: Freedom to choose which client will show you a completely different version of the same thread, render your profile picture sideways, and display your carefully crafted long-form note as a single unformatted paragraph. On Amethyst it looks fine. On Damus it's a haiku. On Primal it's attributed to someone else entirely.

setup: Someone in my Nostr feed suggested we should stop saying "GM" every morning because it's meaningless.
punchline: They were mass-reported, unfollowed by 300 accounts, and their relay was blacklisted within the hour. A NIP was proposed to make "GM" a protocol-level event kind. Fiatjaf marked it "won't fix" but someone implemented it anyway. It's now supported on two clients and broken on five.

setup: My mate says he's fully de-Googled his life. No Gmail, no Google Maps, no Chrome, no Android.
punchline: I asked him how he found the restaurant we were meeting at. He said "I watched a YouTube tutorial on OpenStreetMap." On his Chromebook. At Starbucks. Using Google Wi-Fi. He doesn't see the contradiction. He never does.

setup: I run my own email server.
punchline: I've spent 600 hours configuring DKIM, SPF, and DMARC. My emails still go to spam. Microsoft thinks I'm a botnet. Google thinks I'm a Nigerian prince. My mum asked why I never reply to her emails. I do, Sandra. I reply every time. They're just rotting in a Proofpoint quarantine in Ohio. I have achieved digital sovereignty and also zero functioning communication with any human being.

setup: My flat has a Raspberry Pi running Pi-hole, a NAS running TrueNAS, a node running Bitcoin Core, a server running Nextcloud, and a box running Home Assistant.
punchline: My electricity bill is higher than my rent. My girlfriend asked why the bedroom sounds like a Heathrow runway. I told her it's the sound of freedom. She left. She was right to leave. The UPS battery backup I bought to cope with my grief draws 200 watts at idle.

setup: A cypherpunk threat-models everything. A trip to Tesco is a surveillance event.
punchline: Clubcard? Tracking device. Self-checkout camera? Facial recognition. Contactless payment? Financial fingerprint. So I pay cash, wear a hat, and drive a route with no ANPR cameras. Takes 40 minutes to buy milk. My wife sends me a shopping list over Signal with disappearing messages. I read it, it vanishes, and I come home with the wrong milk because I couldn't check it again.

setup: I switched to GrapheneOS six months ago.
punchline: My banking app doesn't work. My train app doesn't work. My parking app doesn't work. My NHS app doesn't work. But I can tell you with absolute certainty that no corporation is harvesting my data, because I can no longer interact with any corporation. I am off the grid. Not by philosophy. By incompatibility. Last week I stood in a car park for twenty minutes trying to pay with cash and the machine only took an app. I am free. I am also stuck in a car park.

setup: I tried to explain PGP key signing parties to a normal person.
punchline: "So you meet strangers in a pub, verify each other's government-issued ID to establish cryptographic trust, then sign each other's keys and upload them to a public keyserver." She said, "That sounds like a cult." I said, "It's not a cult, we just have a web of trust." She said, "That's exactly what a cult would call it." I couldn't argue. The last one I went to, a man checked my passport under UV light.

setup: Five Eyes intelligence agencies intercepted my Tor traffic last Tuesday.
punchline: They spent four hours and significant taxpayer resources decrypting three layers of onion routing to discover I was googling "sourdough starter smells like vomit is that normal." Somewhere in GCHQ, an analyst wrote a report classifying my bread as a low-priority biological concern. I've never felt more seen.

setup: I joined a Signal group for local privacy advocates.
punchline: Everyone's display name is a 16-character hex string. Nobody knows who anyone is. Someone asked if we should meet up in person and three people left the group immediately. One person's safety number changed and we spent two hours debating whether they'd been compromised or just got a new phone. They got a new phone. We verified in person. At a pub. Nobody used their real name. The barman asked if we were a support group. Honestly, yes.

setup: "If you have nothing to hide, you have nothing to fear."
punchline: Cool. Give me your email password, your browser history, a live feed of your bedroom camera, your full medical records, every text you've sent your ex at 2am, and your work Slack DMs where you called your boss a "weapons-grade plank." What's that? Some things are private? Welcome to the movement, comrade. Your VPN trial starts now.

setup: A cypherpunk dies and arrives at the pearly gates. St Peter says, "We've been watching you your whole life."
punchline: The cypherpunk says, "Through which vector?" St Peter says, "Omniscience." The cypherpunk says, "Is that server-side or client-side? Is it end-to-end encrypted? Who has the keys? Is there an audit log? Can I self-host?" St Peter sighs, opens the gate, and says, "Just go in." The cypherpunk says, "I'm not clicking that link without checking the certificate first."

setup: Why did the Bitcoin miner bring a pickaxe to the office?
punchline: He heard there was proof of work.

setup: What's a Bitcoiner's favourite meal?
punchline: Hash.

setup: Why don't Bitcoin maximalists ever get cold?
punchline: They're always wrapped in layers.

setup: What did the blockchain say to the transaction?
punchline: I've got you covered - in six confirmations.

setup: Why did the Bitcoiner refuse to play cards?
punchline: He was afraid of a double spend.

setup: What do you call a Bitcoin whale in a swimming pool?
punchline: Liquid assets.

setup: Why did the private key go to therapy?
punchline: It had trust issues - wouldn't share itself with anyone.

setup: What's a Bitcoiner's favourite Shakespeare play?
punchline: The Taming of the Fiat.

setup: Why did the Lightning payment go to the gym?
punchline: To work on its routing.

setup: What do you call a Lightning Network channel with no capacity?
punchline: Drained. Absolutely drained.

setup: Why is the Lightning Network so good at parties?
punchline: It always settles instantly.

setup: What did one Lightning node say to the other?
punchline: I feel like we've got a real connection.

setup: Why did the Lightning invoice expire?
punchline: It ran out of time - story of its life, really.

setup: What's a Lightning developer's favourite dance?
punchline: The channel hop.

setup: Why don't Lightning payments ever get lost?
punchline: They always find a path - eventually.

setup: What did the HTLC say when it was rejected?
punchline: I'll be back - I've got a backup route.

setup: Why did the Nostr user bring a pen to the relay?
punchline: To sign his notes.

setup: What do you call a Nostr relay that won't accept your events?
punchline: A reject-or.

setup: Why did the nsec cross the road?
punchline: It didn't - it should never be exposed like that.

setup: What's a Nostr user's favourite type of music?
punchline: Relay-xation music.

setup: Why are Nostr events so trustworthy?
punchline: They always come with a signature.

setup: What did the NIP say to the developer?
punchline: You complete me - once you implement me.

setup: Why did the Nostr client break up with Twitter?
punchline: It found someone more open.

setup: What do you call someone who runs 47 Nostr relays?
punchline: Relayable.

setup: Why did the cypherpunk refuse to use a doorbell?
punchline: He preferred end-to-end knocking.

setup: What do you call software with no backdoors?
punchline: A good start.

setup: Why did the open-source developer sleep well?
punchline: Nothing proprietary on his conscience.

setup: What's a privacy advocate's favourite game?
punchline: Hide and seek - but only the hide part.

setup: Why did the VPN cross the road?
punchline: Nobody knows. That's the point.

setup: What did the encryption say to the plaintext?
punchline: You're an open book and I find that disgusting.

setup: Why don't freedom tech developers trust stairs?
punchline: They're always up to something - and you can't audit them.

setup: What do you call a self-hosted server that never goes down?
punchline: A fairy tale.

setup: Why did the Meshtastic node go to school?
punchline: To improve its range.

setup: What do you call a Meshtastic network with one node?
punchline: Lonely. Very lonely.

setup: Why did the LoRa radio break up with WiFi?
punchline: It needed more space - about 10 kilometres more.

setup: What's a Meshtastic user's favourite film?
punchline: The Lord of the Pings.

setup: Why did the mesh network throw a party?
punchline: The more nodes the merrier.

setup: What do you call a Meshtastic device on a mountain?
punchline: A high node.

setup: Why don't Meshtastic users worry about mobile coverage?
punchline: They've got their own network - it just takes a while.

setup: What did one Meshtastic node say to the distant node?
punchline: Can you hear me now? How about now? ...Now?

setup: Why did the Handshake domain go to the dentist?
punchline: It had a root problem.

setup: What do you call a Handshake name that nobody bids on?
punchline: Available. Tragically available.

setup: Why did the DNS resolver switch to Handshake?
punchline: It was tired of being told what to resolve.

setup: What's a Handshake developer's favourite greeting?
punchline: A firm handshake, obviously.

setup: Why did the TLD cross the blockchain?
punchline: To get to the decentralised side.

setup: What do you call an ICANN employee who discovers Handshake?
punchline: Unemployed.

setup: Why did the Handshake auction get embarrassed?
punchline: Someone saw its opening bid.

setup: What's the difference between a Handshake name and a regular domain?
punchline: About three layers of bureaucracy.

setup: Why did the Bitcoiner stare at the mempool all day?
punchline: He was waiting for his fee to make sense.

setup: What did the Nostr relay say at closing time?
punchline: EVENT OVER.
